summary: O'Neil argues that the Apple vs. FBI encryption debate is fundamentally misdirected. Rather than celebrating Apple as a privacy hero standing up to government overreach, we should be asking why our government isn't protecting our privacy in the first place. Apple is a profit-seeking corporation accountable to shareholders, not citizens, and will comply with laws when forced. The real conversation should be about demanding democratic accountability from government institutions that are making unilateral decisions about privacy trade-offs without public input.
key_points: Framing Apple as 'good' and government as 'bad' concedes too much and oversimplifies a complex democratic problem
Apple is accountable to laws and shareholders, not citizens—they cannot be privacy heroes even if they wanted to be
The FBI is making internal calculations about privacy trade-offs without measuring or communicating the risks to the public
Citizens should demand a government that acknowledges its duty to protect privacy while investigating risks
The focus on Apple's operating systems and technical details is a sideshow distracting from the real democratic governance issues
money_quotes: We are reduced to relying on an enormous, profit seeking corporation to make a stand for our rights.
Apple is not accountable to us. They are a private company.
They are not heroes, because they cannot be, even if they wanted to be.
Right now the FBI is falling far short, trivializing the risk to the rest of us when backdoors are created and used at scale.
They have made an internal calculation that the trade-offs are well worth the risks, without really having a conversation with the public in which they even measure the risks.
And those risks are our risks.
Let's stop talking about Apple and start talking about democracy in the era of big data.
key_insight: When we rely on corporations to protect our rights instead of demanding democratic accountability from government, we've already lost the more important battle.
